---
title: "Internet Appendix for: Your Paper Title"
author:
  - name: Your Name
    email: you@example.org
    affiliations:
      - name: Your Department, Your University
        address: Some Address, Country
        corresponding: true
  - name: Co-Author One
    email: one@example.org
    affiliations:
      - name: Co-Author Department, Co-Author University
        address: Some Address, Country
  - name: Co-Author Two
    email: two@example.org
    affiliations:
      - name: Co-Author Department, Co-Author University
        address: Some Address, Country
date: today
date-format: "[This revision:] D MMMM YYYY"
abstract: |
  This Internet Appendix is a worked typographic example. The
  content below is unrelated to the main paper and is included
  only to show how the template renders longer prose, equations,
  theorem environments, and bibliography references. Replace it
  with material specific to your paper.
bibliography: references-appendix.bib

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    documentclass: article
    classoption:
      - "12pt"
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    linestretch: 2
    indent: true
    fontsize: 12pt
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: red
    citecolor: blue
    urlcolor: magenta
    cite-method: natbib
    biblio-style: apalike
    number-sections: true
    keep-tex: true
    subparagraph: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{amsthm}
        \usepackage{subcaption}
        \usepackage{setspace}
        \usepackage{enumitem}
        \usepackage{multirow}
        \usepackage{longtable}
        \usepackage{tabularx}
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{sectsty}

        \AtBeginEnvironment{tabular}{\onehalfspacing}

        \makeatletter
        \renewcommand{\title}[1]{%
          \gdef\@title{#1\\[0.3cm]%
            {\normalsize\itshape Supplementary Results}%
          }%
        }
        \makeatother

        \subsectionfont{\large\itshape}
        \subsubsectionfont{\large\itshape}
        \makeatletter
        \renewcommand{\subsection}{\@startsection{subsection}{2}{\z@}%
          {-3.25ex \@plus -1ex \@minus -.2ex}{1.5ex \@plus .2ex}%
          {\normalfont\large\itshape}}
        \renewcommand{\subsubsection}{\@startsection{subsubsection}{3}{\z@}%
          {-3.25ex \@plus -1ex \@minus -.2ex}{1.5ex \@plus .2ex}%
          {\normalfont\large\itshape}}
        \makeatother

        \makeatletter
        \def\@seccntformat#1{\@ifundefined{#1@cntformat}%
            {\csname the#1\endcsname\quad}%
            {\csname #1@cntformat\endcsname}%
        }
        \def\section@cntformat{\thesection.\quad}
        \def\subsection@cntformat{\thesubsection \quad}
        \makeatother

        \tolerance=1
        \emergencystretch=\maxdimen
        \hyphenpenalty=10000
        \hbadness=10000

        \let\oldequation=\equation
        \let\endoldequation=\endequation
        \renewenvironment{equation}{%
          \vspace{0.25cm}\begin{oldequation}}{\vspace{0.25cm}\end{oldequation}%
        }

        \renewcommand{\thesection}{Appendix \Alph{section}}
        \renewcommand{\thesubsection}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{subsection}}
        \renewcommand{\thetable}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{table}}
        \renewcommand{\thefigure}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{figure}}
        \renewcommand{\theequation}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{equation}}

        \theoremstyle{plain}
        \newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}[section]
        \newtheorem{proposition}[theorem]{Proposition}
        \newtheorem{lemma}[theorem]{Lemma}
        \theoremstyle{definition}
        \newtheorem{definition}[theorem]{Definition}
        \newtheorem{assumption}[theorem]{Assumption}
---

# What Is a Black Hole? {#sec-app-blackhole}

A black hole is a region of spacetime where gravity is so intense
that nothing, not even light, can escape once it crosses an
imaginary surface called the event horizon. From a long way away
the black hole looks like a small, very dark object; up close, it
is more accurately described as a structure of the geometry of
space itself.

## A Brief History of the Idea {#sec-app-blackhole-history}

The possibility of objects so dense that light cannot escape was
already discussed by John Michell in 1783 and independently by
Laplace shortly afterwards. The modern picture emerged from
Schwarzschild's 1916 solution to Einstein's field equations of
general relativity. As surveyed by @blackhole-review2024,
black-hole physics has since become a central object of study in
both classical and quantum gravity.

# The Schwarzschild Solution {#sec-app-schwarzschild}

Outside a spherically symmetric, non-rotating mass $M$, spacetime
is described by the Schwarzschild metric. In standard coordinates
$(t, r, \theta, \phi)$ and units with $G = c = 1$, the line
element is

$$
ds^{2}
= -\left(1 - \frac{2M}{r}\right) dt^{2}
+ \left(1 - \frac{2M}{r}\right)^{-1} dr^{2}
+ r^{2}\, d\Omega^{2},
$$ {#eq-app-schwarz}

where $d\Omega^{2} = d\theta^{2} + \sin^{2}\theta\, d\phi^{2}$ is
the line element on the unit two-sphere. The radius $r = 2M$ is
the *event horizon*: an infalling observer who crosses it cannot
send any signal back outward.

## The Horizon Area and Surface Gravity {#sec-app-schwarzschild-horizon}

The horizon at $r = 2M$ is a two-sphere of area

$$
A = 4\pi (2M)^{2} = 16\pi M^{2}
$$ {#eq-app-area}

and the associated surface gravity is

$$
\kappa = \frac{1}{4M}.
$$ {#eq-app-kappa}

These two quantities reappear in the next section's discussion of
black-hole thermodynamics.

# Black-Hole Thermodynamics {#sec-app-thermo}

A surprising parallel between the laws governing horizons and the
laws of thermodynamics emerged in the 1970s. Treating the horizon
area as an entropy and the surface gravity as a temperature gives
the *first law of black-hole mechanics*,

$$
dM = \frac{\kappa}{8\pi}\, dA + \Omega_{H}\, dJ + \Phi_{H}\, dQ,
$$ {#eq-app-firstlaw}

where $J$ is angular momentum, $Q$ is electric charge, and
$\Omega_{H}, \Phi_{H}$ are the angular velocity and electrostatic
potential at the horizon.

::: {.theorem}
*(No-hair theorem, informal.)* A stationary, asymptotically flat
black-hole solution of the Einstein-Maxwell equations is uniquely
characterized by its mass $M$, angular momentum $J$, and electric
charge $Q$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The full proof requires a careful analysis of the constraint
equations and the rigidity of the horizon Killing vector field;
see standard references for the details. $\square$
:::

# Outlook {#sec-app-outlook}

This appendix has used black-hole physics purely as a vehicle to
demonstrate the qkit paper template's handling of multi-section
appendices, lettered section numbering, displayed equations with
labels, theorem environments, and a separate bibliography. Replace
this material with the supplementary content your own paper
requires.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
